textPlain: I ditched Disqus for the exact same reason, too many adsThen I built an alternative using free Cloudflare WorkerIt's a simple service that transform comment to JSON, append to .jsonl file, then do a `git push`https://github.com/est/req4cmtThe advantange over Github issue based comment system is that it does not require login of any sort, because Github OAuth login will leak all your repo data to along with your `access_token` to the comment plugin provider.Besides the `git push` works for any remote. You can use github/gitlab or whatever.

The problem is that EU Digital Services Act (DSA) requires large online platforms. I think that disqus might have fallen under "large online platforms". And the problem is when do you become a large platform? Is this promotion occupied by a huge penalty fee you need to pay for not filtering your commenters so far?

I think the concern of "blog comments" is best left to external platforms eg HN, Reddit etcWhat would be more useful would be an automated list of places where the post has been discussed (and maybe pull the top comments from there through API?)
I don't know why a $5 VPS and a random CMS isn't the default for developers blogs. Dead simple, runs for years without maintenance, minimal cost, no ads.

Disqus is a great idea turned into hot garbage by (inevitable) lowest-level monetization. I once worked on a blog with Disqus. Whenever we had a hit, such as an HN front page, horrific ads would be injected into the comments section for that page. Any page without significant traffic had no ads, lulling you into a false sense of security. If a page 'hit' in your off-hours, the ghastly and repulsive ads could be running for a day, or even a weekend.They could be turned off at the time but only on a case-by-case basis. In the end, I got rid of Disqus.
